This is tested manually with both `serve deploy` and `anyscale service rollout` commands. Deployment has the following file structure
```
- service.yaml
- local.yaml
- hello_serve.py
- utils/
    - test.py
```
and the files has the following contents
```
# service.yaml
name: "test-utils-service2"
cluster_env: gene-test-environment:30
ray_serve_config:
  import_path: hello_serve:model
  runtime_env:
    working_dir: "."

# local.yaml
import_path: hello_serve:model

# hello_serve.py
import time
from ray import serve
from starlette.requests import Request
from utils.test import hello


@serve.deployment
class HelloModel:
    def __init__(self):
        hello()

    async def __call__(self, starlette_request: Request) -> None:
        hello()
        return f"{hello()}, {time.time()}"

model = HelloModel.bind()


# test.py
def hello():
    text = "hello_from_utils"
    print(text)
    return text
```
- The `gene-test-environment:30` environment was built from the wheel of this PR.
- Setup a workspace based off this environment and run `serve deploy local.yaml` was able to see the default application deployed successfully in the dashboard. Also was able to run `curl http://localhost:8000` and see the expected response.

I ran the `test_library_MovieSection_managedHubs` test locally and it was failing. I found out that you need to provide all three parameters (`promotedToRecommended`, `promotedToOwnHome`, and `promotedToSharedHome`) in the API call to update the visibility managed hubs, even if you don't change them. Currently `updateVisibility()` only provides the parameters that change instead of all three. This also means the `updateVisibility()` needs to automatically `reload()` the hub so that the default attributes get updated if the method is called again/chained.

This seems fine and the additional io manager seems necessary to avoid switching to using in-memory when using the in-process executor. Ideally, some of the same improvements you've added to `default_job_io_manager_with_fs_io_manager_schema` could be added to the normal default job IO manager, but that can be a separate PR certainly. 

We originally kept using `default_job_io_manager` for the job case to easily power `materialize_to_memory` and testing IIRC, but I'm realizing that it also introduces a weird behavior disparity, which I think was an oversight, for jobs that are created using `define_asset_job` when assets are provided resource or not. 
- If I call define_asset_job and I used `with_resources` on my assets, the default IO manager is `default_job_io_manager_with_fs_io_manager_schema`.
- If I call define_asset_job and did not use `with_resources` on my assets, the default IO manager is `default_job_io_manager`. 
- Perhaps most pressingly (and maybe write a unit test to show this is the case), I think that if you call with_resources on only some of your assets and not others, and then create a job using all of them, it will simply fail to compile because of different resources for the io manager key.

Solving this is probably a breaking change, but should get something sensible here for 1.1. Fine with approving, since this behavior disparity predates this PR, but should consider ways to fix.

Looks good at first glance, I haven't tested the code, though. From reviewing the code, I do have some remarks:
 - You say the stty stuff is no longer needed, but are you sure? AFAICS that code was intended to check whether the Linux side of the connection supported a particular baudrate and then switch the PN532 to the highest baudrate supported by the particular serial port. I think that Linux serial port baudrate support has been spotty in the past (different drivers implemented different APIs and different selections of baudrates), I'm not sure how this is now.
 - With the stty code gone, you now always switch to the hardcoded `max_baudrate`, there is no longer any chance that another baudrate is switched to. Given that, it doesn't make much sense to probe the device with all baudrates (between default and max ) - just the default and max would be sufficient (assuming that you are only trying to recover from a situation where nfcpy was previously used to talk to the device, but if you want to support the case where another process (or a previous nfcpy with a different max_baudrate set) has configured the device, then it makes sense to probe all baudrates, but then I'd think you need really probe all, including the ones below default_baudrate).
 - This relates to #188 but does not really solve all sides of that issue: To disable baudrate changes, I now still need to change the nfcpy sourcecode, it would be nice if there was a way to set the max_baudrate through the connection-making API (but that might be out of scope for this PR, of course).

Looking good I think... I would like it if you could move a few things around to better align with the existing `Fsm`-related architecture of this package.

* Please put move `regex_to_nfa` function to be a method on the `Pattern` class as `Pattern.to_nfa`, a sibling of [`Pattern.to_fsm`](https://github.com/qntm/greenery/blob/7a8fff4473d3a2046f3a33a84f945ad0536565b1/greenery/rxelems.py#L623).
* Please move the `nfa_simulate` function to be a method on the `NFA` class as `NFA.accepts`, corresponding with our existing [`Fsm.accepts`](https://github.com/qntm/greenery/blob/7a8fff4473d3a2046f3a33a84f945ad0536565b1/greenery/fsm.py#L167C9-L167C16). As we go forward my hope is that the API of `NFA` will be almost identical to that of `Fsm`.
* Would it be possible to provide an implementation of `from_dfa` to correspond with [`from_fsm`](https://github.com/qntm/greenery/blob/7a8fff4473d3a2046f3a33a84f945ad0536565b1/greenery/rxelems.py#L269)?

Great start! Super clean, clever code with good practices! This will be a very appreciated feature. Just to know for another time, before we start looking at PRs they should normally:
- have a CHANGELOG.md entry
- pass tests; the one that is currently failing illustrates an issue!
- introduce new tests if the PR adds a new feature, or otherwise more than a non-negligible number of lines of moving parts
- ideally name the branches without '/' as the branch names get reused for Docker image names, and it can be a pain to get Podman to pull the right image with certain special characters in the name.

There is one major issue with the logic still. It does not consider score categories with a `category_aggregation = sum` properly. These need sums of max and min values for all predictors within the category. Consider `protein_prediction` in the demo case: there two protein predictors in the 1.20 model, Sift and Polyphen. Each of those can give one point to the score, so e.g. http://localhost:5000/cust000/643594/4c7d5c70d955875504db72ef8e1abe77 gets score 2 but the range is shown as 0<x<1.

Sorry for not coming across this sooner. I'll leave some more detailed notes on the root issue, but we should keep the author/committer names in the update metadata.

While it is true the author information could be derived from the commit hash, that also means that the Pulumi backend would need to be given access to the repo too. And that, IMHO, seems like the wrong tradeoff. Since the backend -- whether it is app.pulumi.com, or some other implementation -- would benefit from being able to say _who_ committed the code. (But does not need to know the contents of the code itself.)

So rather than just removing the metadata values entirely (since they are useful), it might be more prudent to simply allow an opt-out. For example, omitting all of the `GitCommiter` and `GitAuthor` metadata values if `PULUMI_OMIT_COMMIT_AUTHOR_METADATA` is truthy.

This breaks `certbot update_account -m email@example.com`, because the signature of `update_registration` is:

```python
    def update_registration(self, regr: messages.RegistrationResource,
                            update: Optional[messages.Registration] = None
                            ) -> messages.RegistrationResource:
```

The first argument being the existing `regr`, and  the second argument being the changes you actually want to make.

Unfortunately, `main.update_account` calls `update_registration(regr.update(...))`, instead of `update_registration(regr, regr.body.update(contact=...))`.

The former method is broken by this PR, because `_get_v2_account` overwrites the body of the `regr` (the first argument) with what is sent by the ACME server. This wipes out the change to `contact` queued up by `main.update_account`. 

This is correct behavior in my opinion, but arguably a breaking change to the `acme` module's API.

We could fix the way `main.update_account` calls `update_registration`, but we'd still be potentially breaking other API users who were relying on the old behavior of `update_registration`.

My main comment is that we will now have two properties with a very similar content, which seems wasteful. We will never check these for validity.

Instead, my suggestion would be the following:

1. We only store the relative logdir path (let's call it `relative_logdir`)
2. `Trial.logdir` becomes a [cached property](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functools.html#functools.cached_property) (use [this]{https://pypi.org/project/cached-property/) for python 3.7 compatibility, similar to dataclasses for 3.6)
3. For backwards compatibility, we should extract the relative path when `self.logdir` is set (`@property.setter`), but print a deprecation warning
4. Same for restoring pickled objects (if `state["logdir"]` is set, just call `self.logdir = state.pop("logdir")`

We should add (very short and simple) unit tests to show that these conversions work.

It would also be great to see a test where we move an experiment analysis directory and recover the same information, showing that relative path lookup works.

Let me know if you have bandwidth to add this, otherwise I'm happy to chime in!

At this point I think it is appropriate to step back and thing about what we are modeling here. The terminology here around deep and shallow is quite subtle and I think at this point misleading and confusing. I don't know when it is appropriate to call one version versus another.

`_as_config_dict_shallow`: This is enforcing the following rules:

* Skipping internal fields (This step should be gone soon once we land lifecycle methods)
* Skipping optional fields that are none
* Remapping aliases.
* All first-level values in the dictionary are preserved

So this is used in the context where one is only manipulating `keys` or the structure of the dictionary. One could argue it should be a separate function that maps old key names to new key names and return that data structure.

As it is, this function returns a dictionary that is in a weird intermediate state where the _structure_ of dictionary has changed, but the values are not serializable/directly usable. It is up to the programmer to understand this, which is asking a lot.

`_as_config_dict_deep`

Does the dictionary restructuring _and_ then processes it so that it can be consumed by the underlying configuration system. This also returns the representation that is persisted with runs and passed to the UI, etc.

Step 1: structure the keys
Step 2: process the restructured tree

So broadly I think we need to have cleaner terminology here and then structure the code to match that terminology so that our future selves understand what is going on.

Since I personally never use `get_neighbors_by_distance`, I'm fine with any implementation. I just want to say that if the goal is to get a good number of neighbors, this algorithm is probably a bit too expensive, because it internally runs `_get_neighbors` possibly multiple times (which itself is a relatively expensive function). Besides, there's still no guarantee that you get the correct number of neighbors in the end, since `_get_neighbors` itself relies on `_get_boundary_layer_width`, which estimates the width required from the density. In total, what in my opinion makes the most sense is to estimate the number of atoms from the global density (or Voronoi, if you really want to be sure), i.e. it should be:

```
num_neighbors = (1+buffer)*length**3/volume_per_atom
```

And maybe it's up to the user to determine `buffer`.

First, this looks fantastic. This was a huge refactor to undertake, and you've done great work with it!

Aside from the line comments below, where things caught my eye, I mostly have higher-level concerns.

First, we learned recently that performing `alter` operations on several tables leaves the earlier tables locked while performing the later alters.  In [that case](https://github.com/taskcluster/taskcluster/blob/5e31f1a03bfcc685149a53231660ab25e5e5be1c/db/versions/0049.yml#L5-L25) it was simply dropping a column, but one of those tables was already locked and the upgrade hung waiting for that lock -- and holding lots more locks, causing an outage.  So the lesson we learned was to try to split "alter" operations on many tables up into different DB versions.  In this case, I'd suggest splitting the PR up into two PRs: one for worker info (provisioners / worker types / workers) and one for tasks.  Having read through this PR, I think that such a split will be pretty quick, and will certainly help to compartmentalize the reviews.  But if it is hard to split, it's fine to keep it all in a single PR (with two DB revisions).

For the worker-info changes, it seems that the "provisioners" bit has yet to be done.  The existing architecture defines a hierarchy (provisioners, worker types, workers) and I think the new model defines a shorter hierarchy (task queues, workers).  The `listProvisioners`, `getProvisioner`, `listWorkerTypes`, and `getWorkerType` can be marked as deprecated and emulated from this new hierarchy.  It would make sense to add `listTaskQueues` and `getTaskQueue`, too (without an `action` property).  The existing `declareProvisioner` and `declareWorkerType` methods can be deprecated and stubbed out to do nothing, as to my knowledge they are unused.  And, I don't think we need a new `declareTaskQueue` method.  We can make a followup issue to rewrite the UI to use the new methods (I'm not sure this is worth the effort).

For the task changes, we have also learned a painful lesson: deployments of Taskcluster have millions of tasks, and as-written the migration would take tens of minutes, causing a service outage.  We've had some ideas about how to solve this kind of problem in #3139.  I think an [alter table .. add column .. default null](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/47709593/postgresql-add-column-default-null-locks-and-performance) can run quickly regardless of number of rows, so maybe that's the right approach. It unifies `<orm>_get_or_create()` under a single option. Having the `unique_constraints` matching Django `unique_together` facilitates writing `unique_constraints` for Django. `unique_constraints` are more powerful than `get_or_create`, they grant more controls over the lookup strategy. Removing the junk data generated when resolving the factory declarations is just great.

I’m sure users will be thankful for the very clear migration path. It would be nice to provide a deadline for removing the compatibility schemes for `get_or_create`. Perhaps for version 4.0?

IMO, the following commits should be squashed. They all have a single purpose, adding the `unique_constraints` feature. Splitting them was helpful for the review :+1:.

- Expose memleak in SQLAlchemyModelFactory.
- Add an optional normalization to class Meta fields
- Provide support for migrating class Meta fields
- Add `unique_constraints` and associated _lookup()
- Replace *_get_or_create with unique_constraints.

---

As far as I’m concerned, `get_or_create` (or `unique_constraints`) should not be used in tests. When writing a test, the exact state of the database should be known and the dev should know whether the data is to be created or if it’s already present in the DB and should be retrieved. But users rely on that feature, and getting it an upgrade is likely beneficial to them.

---

High level comment for the group - do we want to support the following - 
- [Fast and Accurate Model Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.06877) proposes a way to scale models. Once a model is scaled, it cannot be defined by `w_a`, `w_m`, etc. anymore and instead needs to be specified by the stage widths, group widths, etc. which means the `RegNetParams` API does not suffice. We already have models like these being trained currently with multiple downstream applications (happy to share details internally). In Classy Vision we had an `AnyNetParams` API for this (https://github.com/facebookresearch/ClassyVision/blob/b9ea86b39cc469867aabb76ada33bf5a8530eb19/classy_vision/models/anynet.py#L364), which `RegNetParams` derived from.
- The [Designing Network Design Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2003.13678) paper specified AnyNets as fairly general networks and `RegNetX`, `RegNetY`, `RegNetZ` are instantiations of these. Which is why we have multiple block types - a user can specify very general networks with these settings. 

IMO at the very least we should support instantiating scaled models. Supporting general AnyNets might be out of scope for torchvision, I'm not sure (they're quite useful too). Answering these two questions is important to answer some of the comments in code currently!

i think removing this line here will have the same effect: https://github.com/localstack/localstack/blob/61a1bbcb9a70be6d4ced28fc979ea68654c02acf/Dockerfile#L155

* make install does a `pip install -e`, which creates in the virtual environment an egg-link file to the project itself
* `pip uninstall localstack` removes that egg link (i have no idea why we/I introduced this line)
* when we then run `pip install --upgrade localstack-ext`, localstack is resolved again because it is no longer resolved

if we remove the `pip uninstall localstack` line, then:
* `pip install --upgrade localstack-ext` will happily find the localstack requirement to be satisfied (the egg-link created by `pip install -e`) and will no longer overwrite localstack

I can only review the Rust code (rather than the big picture of how it fits into Pants). I've also not looked through all the 'resolved' comments, so I might've been repeating some things.

My general comment is: nice!

Broadly speaking though, it feels like there's quite a lot of `unwrap`ing. I've commented on some specific ones, but there's also a lot of `name.named_child(...).unwrap()` or equivalent. I'd be nervous about this making dep inference fragile to invalid code, or syntax changes, or other unexpected problems, and bringing down the whole pantsd when they fail.

Some alternatives, depending on the situation:

- propagate errors with `?` etc.
- `.expect("<short explanation>")` or `unwrap_or_with(|| panic!("foo {some_value} bar"))` to at least give more context
- ignore silently or with some logging (e.g. if the code is invalid)

Trying this out locally - ran into a few blocking issues.

First - an issue with stack exports.  If I deploy this:

```ts
import * as aws from "@pulumi/aws";
const bucket = new aws.s3.Bucket("my-bucket", {});
export const bucketName = bucket.id;
```

Then run `pulumi preview --save-plan plan.json` then immediately `pulumi up --plan plan.json`, I get:

```
➜  simple PULUMI_EXPERIMENTAL=true /opt/pulumi/bin/pulumi preview --save-plan plan.json
Previewing update (dev)

View Live: https://app.pulumi.com/lukehoban/simple-foo/dev/previews/66981c6d-e229-45ae-a086-a010d3e0c0b5

     Type                 Name            Plan
     pulumi:pulumi:Stack  simple-foo-dev
     └─ aws:s3:Bucket     my-bucket

Resources:
    2 unchanged

➜  simple PULUMI_EXPERIMENTAL=true /opt/pulumi/bin/pulumi up --plan plan.json
Previewing update (dev)

View Live: https://app.pulumi.com/lukehoban/simple-foo/dev/previews/f6898a61-2352-40cc-a9f9-2104b6ce8291

     Type                 Name            Plan        Info
 ~   pulumi:pulumi:Stack  simple-foo-dev  refresh     1 error
 ~   └─ aws:s3:Bucket     my-bucket       refresh

Diagnostics:
  pulumi:pulumi:Stack (simple-foo-dev):
    error: resource violates plan: properties changed: -bucketName


error: Expected resource operations for urn:pulumi:dev::simple-foo::pulumi:pulumi:Stack::simple-foo-dev but none were seen.
```

Second - an issue that I suspect is due to the refresh piece (I have notes in the review feedback that I think we need to decouple this - but if we are considering keeping this, we'll need to ensure it doesn't trigger the issue here):

If I deploy this:

```ts
import * as aws from "@pulumi/aws";
const bucket = new aws.s3.Bucket("my-bucket", {});
```

And then change it to:

```ts
```ts
import * as aws from "@pulumi/aws";
const bucket = new aws.s3.Bucket("my-bucket", {
    website: {
        indexDocument: "index.html",
    },
});
```

Then run `pulumi preview --save-plan plan.json` then immediately `pulumi up --plan plan.json`, I get:

```
➜  simple PULUMI_EXPERIMENTAL=true /opt/pulumi/bin/pulumi up --plan plan.json
Previewing update (dev)

View Live: https://app.pulumi.com/lukehoban/simple-foo/dev/previews/cf933799-a8ec-4d61-9fe6-a73232576220

     Type                 Name            Plan        Info
     pulumi:pulumi:Stack  simple-foo-dev
 ~   └─ aws:s3:Bucket     my-bucket       refresh     1 error

Diagnostics:
  aws:s3:Bucket (my-bucket):
    error: resource violates plan: properties changed: -acl, -websiteEndpoint, -bucketDomainName, -requestPayer, -bucketRegionalDomainName, -corsRules, -tags, -region, -lifecycleRules, -versioning, -tagsAll, -bucket, -loggings, -websiteDomain, -id, -forceDestroy, -accelerationStatus, -grants, -hostedZoneId, -arn, +website


error: Expected resource operations for urn:pulumi:dev::simple-foo::aws:s3/bucket:Bucket::my-bucket but none were seen.
```

All of these changed properties are results of the refresh modifying the state, but don't seem to be captured into the plan, so that the plan doesn't actually capture what the combined operation is going to do, so the corresponding update can't work.
